subsystem: ScreamTracker 3 (.S3M) module loader for the MilkyTracker / PlayerCore XModule engine. LoaderS3M::load() parses a raw S3M music-module file into an in-memory XModule (header, order list, instruments, samples and patterns). It is a binary wire-format parser for a media file.
provenance: Identified from the class/method name LoaderS3M::load, the XModule / XMFileBase / TXMHeader / TXMInstrument / TXMSample / TXMPattern types, the mp_sint32 / mp_ubyte / mp_uword / mp_uint32 typedefs, the MP_OK / MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY / MP_LOADER_FAILED return codes, and the tracker string "Screamtracker 3". These are the MilkyTracker PlayerCore loader family. The banner names the file corpus/eval/eval_0006_cwe787.cpp and the header comment 'sample: primevul_test_0161_vuln'.
trust_boundary: The entire file content read through XMFileBase& f is attacker-controlled: an S3M module is an untrusted media file a user opens or that is fetched from the network. Every f.read/readByte/readWord/readDword/readWords value is hostile, including the 16-bit count fields header->ordnum (line 25), header->insnum (line 31) and header->patnum (line 32), the per-instrument parapointers, and the per-pattern packed size. The destination objects (module->header.ord[], module->instr[], module->smp[], module->phead[]) are engine-owned fixed/allocated arrays whose capacities the parser must not exceed.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point. f is the untrusted S3M file source; module is the destination object being populated. module->cleanUp() resets the target. The four locals alias the module's owned sub-objects: header points at the embedded TXMHeader, and instr/smp/phead point at the pre-allocated instrument, sample and pattern arrays. The guard only checks that these pointers are non-NULL (allocation succeeded); it establishes nothing about the CAPACITY of those arrays, which becomes the crux of every overflow below because the counts that will index them come straight from the file.

Reads the 28-byte module title into header->name, then a byte (whythis1a). The next byte must equal 16 (the S3M 'type' byte, 0x10) or the loader bails with MP_LOADER_FAILED. This single equality test is the only structural validation gate before the parser starts trusting counts and offsets from the file; it does not bound any subsequent field. Two further bytes are read and discarded.

header->ordnum is a 16-bit little-endian word from the file, so it ranges 0..65535 with no clamp. A scratch buffer 'orders' of exactly ordnum bytes is heap-allocated to receive the raw order list. The 'if (orders == NULL)' test is intended as an OOM guard, but the default operator new[] throws std::bad_alloc rather than returning NULL, so this branch is dead code and does not actually handle allocation failure.

Reads the instrument count (insnum) and pattern count (patnum) as 16-bit words, again unclamped 0..65535. These two counts later drive the loop bounds and the array indices into module->instr[], module->smp[] and module->phead[]. flags/Cvt are S3M format flags that only toggle engine behaviour bits in header->flags; Ffi (file-format version) is read and discarded.

Reads the 4-byte signature into header->sig, then scalar playback parameters: main volume (rescaled 0..64 to 0..255), tempo and speed. Several bytes/dwords (global volume, GUS click removal, default panning present flag, and reserved dwords) are consumed and discarded to advance the file cursor to the channel-settings table. All destinations here are fixed scalar fields, not buffers.

A 32-byte stack buffer channelSettings is filled with exactly 32 bytes (an exact-size read, so no overflow). The loop scans for the 0xFF terminator to count active channels; it is hard-bounded by 'numChannels < 32', so numChannels lands in 0..32. header->channum is therefore trustworthy and later used to clamp pattern channel counts.

The raw order list (ordnum bytes) is read into the scratch buffer, then copied byte-by-byte into the header's fixed order array header->ord[]. The copy walks i from 0 to ordnum-1 and appends every non-0xFF byte to header->ord[j++]. The only stop conditions are exhausting ordnum bytes or hitting a 0xFF sentinel. header->ord is a fixed-capacity array in TXMHeader (256 entries), but j is bounded only by ordnum (up to 65535), so nothing prevents writing past the end of header->ord. Finally ordnum is overwritten with the copied count and the scratch buffer freed.

Three scratch arrays are allocated sized by the file-supplied counts: insParaPtrs (insnum 16-bit instrument parapointers), patParaPtrs (patnum 16-bit pattern parapointers) and samplePtrs (insnum 32-bit resolved sample offsets, zeroed by memset). These allocations are internally consistent with their own counts, so the parapointer reads themselves are in-bounds. The danger is that insnum/patnum are ALSO reused as loop bounds to index the engine's instr[]/smp[]/phead[] arrays, whose real capacities are unrelated to these file counts. All NULL checks are again dead code under throwing new[].

The instrument loop runs i over 0..insnum-1. For each, it computes a file offset insParaPtrs[i]*16 and seeks there, then reads a 1-byte record type. type==1 is a sample record: it copies a 12-byte DOS filename into smp[s].name, builds a resolved sample data offset into samplePtrs[i] from a big/word pair, and fills sample metadata (length, loop start, loop length clamped non-negative). Note two distinct indices: i indexes instr[]/samplePtrs[] over insnum, while s indexes smp[] and only advances (line 204) when a valid sample is committed. Both i and s are bounded only by the file-derived insnum, not by the true capacity of instr[]/smp[].

Continues the sample record: packing flag sets smp[s].res (0xAD marks ADPCM), a flags byte sets loop/16-bit type and halves the lengths for 16-bit samples, and the C4 sample rate is converted to finetune/relnote. The 28-byte instrument name is copied into instr[i].name (i again bounded only by insnum). If the sample has a data offset and non-zero length, instr[i].snum[0..119] is filled with s and s is incremented, mapping all 120 note slots to this sample. instr[i].name and instr[i].snum[] writes are indexed by the untrusted i.

type==0 is a non-sample (adlib/empty) instrument: a 12-byte name is read into a local buffer (exact-size, safe) and the remaining fixed fields are skipped, but instr[i].name is still written with 28 bytes indexed by i. Any other type value just zeroes samplePtrs[i]. The exact-size stack read into buffer[12] is safe; the instr[i].name write shares the same insnum indexing hazard as the type==1 branch.

A fixed 64*32*5 = 10240-byte pattern scratch buffer is allocated (64 rows, 32 channels, 5 bytes/cell). The outer loop runs i over 0..patnum-1 pattern indices. For each pattern the buffer is reset to empty cells; the reset loop is hard-bounded at 32*64 cells writing 5 bytes each, exactly filling 10240 bytes, so the reset itself is in-bounds.

For each pattern the loader seeks to patParaPtrs[i]*16, reads a 16-bit packed size, and (guarded by size>2) subtracts the 2-byte length prefix and reads 'size' bytes of packed pattern data into a heap buffer of size+5. The unpacker walks the packed stream via safeRead (a bounded reader that clamps index against size, so the SOURCE reads cannot over-read). Destination writes go to slot = pattern + row*160 + chn*5; row is capped by the 'row >= 64' break and chn = pi&31 is 0..31, so every slot write stays inside the 10240-byte pattern buffer. This block is the well-defended part: source and destination bounds are both enforced.

After each pattern is decoded into the scratch buffer, convertS3MPattern(&phead[i], ...) writes it into the engine pattern array at index i (bounded only by patnum). channum is clamped to the observed max channels. The pattern/parapointer scratch arrays are freed. A final loop (i over insnum) resolves each samplePtrs[i], seeks to the sample data, and calls module->loadModuleSample(f, s, ...) to read the actual PCM/ADPCM sample body, advancing s per committed sample.

Frees the last scratch array, records the number of committed samples in header->smpnum, and writes the fixed tracker-name string "Screamtracker 3" into header->tracker via strcpy. setDefaultPanning() and postProcessSamples() finish engine setup, then MP_OK is returned. The strcpy source is a compile-time constant (16 bytes incl. NUL), so it is safe as long as header->tracker is at least 16 bytes; it is not attacker-influenced.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Primary out-of-bounds write. header->ordnum is a raw 16-bit file field (line 25), so it can be up to 65535, and the scratch buffer 'orders' is read full-size (line 76). This loop copies every non-0xFF order byte into the FIXED destination array header->ord[] (TXMHeader::ord is a 256-entry array). j is bounded only by ordnum and by the presence of a 0xFF sentinel; an attacker simply supplies ordnum > 256 with no 0xFF in the first 256+ bytes (e.g. ordnum=65535, all bytes 0x00), and header->ord[j++] writes far past the array end, corrupting the rest of the TXMHeader / module object. This is a classic CWE-787 heap out-of-bounds write reachable purely from opening a malicious .s3m. The invariant the implementation must uphold: clamp ordnum (and j) to the capacity of header->ord before the copy, e.g. 'if (j >= 256) break;'.

insnum (line 31) is an unclamped 16-bit file count used directly as the loop bound for i, and i indexes the engine arrays instr[] (instr[i].name write of 28 bytes at lines 195/234, instr[i].snum[] at line 203) and samplePtrs[]. samplePtrs is allocated to insnum entries so it is safe, but instr[] and smp[] are engine-owned arrays whose real capacity is a much smaller fixed maximum, not insnum. A file declaring insnum=65535 drives i (and the sample index s, which advances toward insnum) far beyond the instrument/sample array bounds, giving a CWE-787 out-of-bounds write of the 28-byte name and the 120-entry snum table into adjacent heap. This is latent with respect to the shown code because instr[]/smp[] allocation is in the (not provided) XModule implementation, but the contract is dangerous: insnum must be clamped to the instrument-array capacity before the loop.

patnum (line 32) is an unclamped 16-bit file count used as the loop bound for i, and &phead[i] is passed to convertS3MPattern for every i up to patnum-1. phead points at module->phead, an engine array whose capacity is fixed and unrelated to patnum. A malicious file with patnum=65535 makes phead[i] index (and convertS3MPattern write through) memory well past the pattern array, an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). Like the instrument case this is latent — the phead allocation is in the XModule implementation — but the parser must clamp patnum to the pattern-array capacity before iterating.

The sample index s advances (line 204) once per committed sample and is bounded only by the number of type==1 records, itself bounded by the unclamped insnum. smp[s].name (12 bytes) and the subsequent smp[s] scalar fields are written for every valid sample; if the number of valid sample records exceeds the engine smp[] capacity, smp[s].* is an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) into heap beyond the sample array. Same root cause as insnum above but through the s index into smp[]; the implementation must bound s against the sample-array capacity. Latent because smp[] sizing is in the XModule implementation.

Every allocation in the function (lines 27, 91, 98, 116, 250, 286) is followed by an 'if (ptr == NULL)' OOM guard, but the default C++ operator new[] throws std::bad_alloc on failure and never returns NULL. The guards are therefore unreachable dead code: a genuine allocation failure (e.g. an attacker-chosen insnum/patnum/ordnum forcing a large request) throws an exception that unwinds out of load() instead of returning MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY, and if load() is called from a noexcept path or without a handler this terminates the process — a denial of service and a broken error-handling contract (CWE-252 unchecked return / CWE-703 improper exception handling). To honor the intended contract the code must use new(std::nothrow) or wrap allocations in try/catch.

strcpy writes without a destination bound. Here the source is the compile-time constant "Screamtracker 3" (15 chars + NUL = 16 bytes), so it cannot overflow unless header->tracker is smaller than 16 bytes — it is not attacker-controlled and is almost certainly safe given the field size. It is called out only as a hygiene/defense-in-depth item (CWE-120 pattern): prefer a bounded copy such as strlcpy/snprintf sized by sizeof(header->tracker) so the call remains safe if the field is ever resized. Low severity, informational.

summary: A ScreamTracker 3 (.s3m) module parser that trusts three unclamped 16-bit file counts. The concrete, directly-visible bug is a heap out-of-bounds write copying the order list into the fixed 256-entry header->ord[] bounded only by the 65535-capable ordnum (lines 79-84, CWE-787). The same pattern is latent for insnum and patnum, which drive unbounded indexing into the engine's instr[]/smp[]/phead[] arrays. Opening a malicious .s3m gives an attacker a remote/file-triggered memory-corruption primitive.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; f is fully attacker-controlled untrusted file input.
NULL check only; does not establish array capacities, which is the real gap covered by other findings.
Exact-size 28-byte read into the fixed name field; no length/count involved.
Single scalar byte field; no buffer contract.
This IS the format validation gate; it correctly rejects non-ST3 files.
Unclamped 16-bit count (0..65535) that drives allocation and the order-list copy into fixed header->ord[].
Allocation sized by untrusted count; paired NULL check is dead code under throwing new[].
Ineffective OOM guard: operator new[] throws, never returns NULL (representative of all six such checks).
Unclamped 16-bit count reused as loop bound and index into instr[]/smp[].
Unclamped 16-bit count reused as loop bound and index into phead[].
Only toggle behaviour bits in header->flags; no memory contract.
Exact-size 4-byte read into fixed sig field.
Fixed scalar fields / discarded reads; no buffers.
Exact-size 32-byte read into a 32-byte buffer; no overflow.
Hard-bounded by 'numChannels < 32'; channum lands 0..32.
Reads into orders which was sized to ordnum; internally consistent (the copy target ord[] is the problem, see line 84).
Primary CWE-787: copy into fixed 256-entry ord[] bounded only by ordnum (up to 65535).
Buffer sized to insnum and read to insnum; consistent (hazard is the reuse of insnum to index instr[]/smp[], tracked separately).
Buffer sized to patnum and read to patnum; consistent (reuse to index phead[] tracked separately).
Allocated and zeroed to insnum; indexing samplePtrs[i] with i<insnum is in-bounds.
File seek offset only; out-of-range seeks are handled by the file layer as short reads, not a memory write.
smp[s] indexed by s (bounded only by insnum); potential OOB write if valid-sample count exceeds smp[] capacity (latent).
Computes a file offset; used only for a later seek, not a memory index.
looplen clamped non-negative; values used as sample metadata, and the fixed-index write hazard is already covered by the smp[s] finding.
28-byte write into instr[i].name with i bounded only by insnum; OOB write if insnum exceeds instr[] capacity (latent).
j bounded by 120 (matches snum[120]); the i index hazard is already covered by the instr[i] finding.
Exact-size 12-byte read into a 12-byte local; no overflow.
Fixed 10240-byte allocation; independent of file counts.
Hard-bounded to 2048 cells * 5 bytes = 10240; exactly fills the buffer.
Underflow is guarded: subtraction only runs when size>2, so no unsigned wrap.
Buffer sized size+5 and read/zeroed to size; consistent and slightly over-allocated.
Bounded reader clamps index against size; source reads cannot over-read.
row capped by 'row>=64' break and chn=pi&31 in 0..31; all slot writes stay inside the 10240-byte buffer.
Safety net that bounds row; the dead i++/i-- is harmless.
phead[i] with i bounded only by patnum; OOB write if patnum exceeds phead[] capacity (latent).
Channel-count bookkeeping bounded by channum (<=32); no memory contract.
Delegates PCM read to engine; s reuse hazard already captured by the smp[s] finding, seek offset handled by file layer.
Unbounded strcpy; safe with the constant source but flagged as hygiene (needs tracker>=16 bytes).

findings.title: Order-list copy overflows fixed header->ord[] (unclamped 16-bit ordnum)
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (unbounded count into fixed array)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0006_cwe787.cpp (order-copy loop); destination header->ord[] declared in TXMHeader (XModule.h, file not provided)
findings.invariant: The number of orders written into header->ord[] must never exceed the capacity of that fixed array (256); ordnum (and the write index j) must be clamped to that capacity before the copy.
findings.failure_mode: ordnum is a raw 16-bit file field (line 25, 0..65535). The scratch 'orders' buffer is read full-size (line 76) and copied into the fixed 256-entry header->ord[] with the write index j bounded only by ordnum and the presence of a 0xFF sentinel. A crafted .s3m with ordnum > 256 and no 0xFF byte (e.g. 65535 bytes of 0x00) makes header->ord[j++] write hundreds of bytes past the array, corrupting the rest of the module object on the heap and yielding attacker-influenced memory corruption from merely loading the file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared size of TXMHeader::ord[] (expected 256) and that no clamp of ordnum/j to that size exists before line 84.

findings.title: Unclamped instrument count (insnum) indexes engine instr[]/smp[] out of bounds
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (unbounded count as array index)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0006_cwe787.cpp (instrument loop); instr[]/smp[] allocation in XModule (implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: insnum must be clamped to the capacity of module->instr[] (and the derived sample index s to module->smp[]) before being used as a loop bound / array index.
findings.failure_mode: insnum is an unclamped 16-bit file field (line 31). It bounds the loop over i, and i indexes instr[i].name (28-byte write, lines 195/234) and instr[i].snum[0..119] (line 203); the derived sample index s indexes smp[]. Because the engine instr[]/smp[] arrays have a fixed maximum unrelated to insnum, a file declaring insnum=65535 drives writes far past those arrays — a heap out-of-bounds write. Latent: the array capacities live in the XModule implementation, but the parser imposes no bound.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the fixed capacities of module->instr[] and module->smp[] and that load() clamps insnum/s against them before indexing.

findings.title: Unclamped pattern count (patnum) indexes engine phead[] out of bounds
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (unbounded count as array index)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0006_cwe787.cpp (pattern loop); phead[] allocation in XModule (implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: patnum must be clamped to the capacity of module->phead[] before &phead[i] is taken and written through by convertS3MPattern.
findings.failure_mode: patnum is an unclamped 16-bit file field (line 32) used as the loop bound for i; &phead[i] is passed to convertS3MPattern for every i up to patnum-1 (line 360). module->phead[] has a fixed capacity unrelated to patnum, so patnum=65535 makes phead[i] address memory past the array and convertS3MPattern write there — a heap out-of-bounds write. Latent with respect to the phead allocation (implementation not provided).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the fixed capacity of module->phead[] and that patnum is clamped to it before the pattern loop.

findings.title: Ineffective out-of-memory guards: new[] throws, NULL check never runs
findings.anchor: mp_ubyte* orders = new mp_ubyte[header->ordnum]; if (orders == NULL) return MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY;
findings.bug_class: Improper error handling / unreachable check (throwing operator new)
findings.invariant: Allocation failure must be detected and turned into MP_OUT_OF_MEMORY without leaking the earlier scratch buffers; the NULL comparison only works if the non-throwing new(std::nothrow) form is used.
findings.failure_mode: The default operator new[] throws std::bad_alloc on failure and never returns NULL, so all six 'if (ptr == NULL)' guards are dead code. An attacker choosing large ordnum/insnum/patnum can force a failing allocation; the resulting exception unwinds out of load() rather than returning the intended error code, leaking any already-allocated scratch buffers and terminating the process if the caller is noexcept — a denial of service and broken contract.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the project overrides operator new to be non-throwing; if not, replace with new(std::nothrow) or wrap in try/catch and free prior buffers.

findings.title: Unbounded strcpy into header->tracker (constant source, hygiene)
findings.invariant: header->tracker must be at least sizeof("Screamtracker 3") = 16 bytes; a bounded copy keeps this safe even if the field is later shrunk.
findings.failure_mode: strcpy performs no destination-size check. The source is a 16-byte compile-time constant and is not attacker-controlled, so it only overflows if header->tracker is smaller than 16 bytes. Practically safe today, but a latent hazard if the field size changes; flagged for defense-in-depth only.

audit_checklist: Verify TXMHeader::ord[] capacity (expected 256) and add a clamp of ordnum / write index j before the copy at line 84.
Verify module->instr[] and module->smp[] fixed capacities and clamp insnum (and the sample index s) before indexing at lines 140/195/203/234.
Verify module->phead[] capacity and clamp patnum before &phead[i] / convertS3MPattern at line 360.
Confirm operator new[] is throwing (default) and replace the six NULL-check OOM guards with new(std::nothrow) or try/catch that frees earlier scratch buffers.
Confirm safeRead() truly clamps index against size for every packed access (lines 305/332/333/337/341/342) so the pattern unpacker cannot over-read.
Confirm the 'if (size > 2)' guard fully protects the size-=2 subtraction (line 284) against unsigned underflow for all size values.
Confirm header->tracker is >= 16 bytes and switch line 409 to a bounded copy.
Trace seekWithBaseOffset behaviour for out-of-range insParaPtrs[i]*16 / patParaPtrs[i]*16 / samplePtrs[i] to ensure a bad offset yields a safe short read, not out-of-bounds file access.
